chapters.title: Most back pain does not start in the spine
chapters.text:
To grasp Tension Myoneural Syndrome, you have to look at the mental side of the problem. Most doctors miss the emotional piece, and that is why they offer the wrong advice for back trouble.

The discomfort from TMS feels physical, but the deeper cause lives in the emotions.

Tension is one such emotion. It comes from feelings you have buried because they feel unacceptable, and those buried feelings feed TMS. Stress is another one, and it is different from tension.

Stress comes from any load that strains you. Money, work, family, bills, and other demands all count. Stress can come from outside, like your job, your health, your relatives, your duties, your bills, and so on. Or it can come from inside, where harder things live, like low self-worth, the need to be perfect, putting things off, and so forth.

As John Sarno wrote, medicine must rethink what causes disease once it accepts how much the emotions matter.

The conscious mind plays a big role in TMS. It is the part of your mind you know about and can steer. It holds the traits you choose and the habits you allow. One common trait in people with TMS is being argumentative, which leads to friction. Knowing you do this can help. Often, the deeper cause sits in the unconscious mind. Most of what you do each day is shaped by thoughts and feelings you have hidden away.

As John Sarno also wrote, nothing pulls attention away from emotional problems like a bit of physical pain.

chapters.title: TMS only makes sense once you see the mental layer
chapters.text:
TMS cannot be understood without looking at the mental side. Most physicians do not see the emotional root, so they hand out the wrong guidance for back pain.

Although TMS shows up as physical pain, the sharp lasting discomfort is mostly emotional.

Tension is one of the emotions involved. It grows from feelings you have pushed away because they feel wrong to have, and those feelings help create TMS. Stress is a separate emotion, and it also plays a part.

Stress differs from tension. It comes from any number of pressures that weigh on you. Money trouble counts. So does anything else that strains you mentally. Stress can arrive from outside, from your job, your health, your relatives, your duties, your bills, and so on. It can also arrive from inside, where deeper things hide, like low self-worth, perfectionism, putting things off, and so forth.

Sarno argues that medicine has to rethink the cause of illness once it takes the emotional side seriously.

Your conscious mind matters for TMS. It is the part you can watch and direct. It carries the traits you pick and the habits you let stand. Argumentativeness is a common trait in people with TMS, and it pulls them into fights. Spotting the trait helps. Still, the deeper driver is the unconscious mind. Most of what you do every day comes from thoughts and feelings you have locked away.

Sarno also pointed out that a little pain is a handy way for the mind to dodge emotional trouble.

chapters.title: TMS hides because it shares signs with other illnesses and lives below awareness
chapters.text:
The feelings that make someone prone to TMS sit below the level of the conscious mind, so the conscious mind cannot catch them. Even so, they shape the traits that run a person day to day. A person with TMS often shows several clear traits. They may overcompensate for a flaw, hold themselves in low regard, push hard for success while fearing failure, live with anger and crushing worry, lean toward self-centeredness, and force feelings down.

These traits matter because some other illnesses look like TMS. Pre-ulcer, hiatus hernia, asthma, spastic colon, tension headache, dizziness, and many others fall in that group. These conditions can show pain that resembles TMS, but they do not come with the mental layer.

The mind drives the start of TMS more than most physicians admit, since they prefer to point to a physical cause. The pain usually ties to a deep fear of something unrelated. As long as the mind keeps connecting the pain to that event, the pain will keep showing up. That is why it helps to look at the physical side too.

The mental side is clear. What about the physical side? TMS starts in the brain, where the rough emotions sit bottled up. The brain then signals the body through the autonomic nervous system, the part that runs your breathing, your heart rate, and where oxygen goes.

When you freeze in fear or ache from TMS, the autonomic nervous system is doing the work.

Since TMS shows up in the body, the autonomic nervous system is fully in the loop. Its answer to tension, stress, or buried feeling is to cut down the flow of fresh blood to the muscles and nerves, which causes pain. So even though TMS lives in your head as a product of the unconscious mind, it produces real physical pain through the autonomic nervous system.

chapters.title: Standard diagnoses and treatments for TMS often miss the point
chapters.text:
To treat something as layered as TMS, you have to think in mental terms first. When pain shows up, the move is to look at the emotional triggers rather than the sore spot.

Changing the wiring of the unconscious mind takes time. Watching the clock only deepens the pain. A few habits can make the healing easier, and they include talking back to your brain, returning to normal activity, dropping structural treatments, and reviewing daily reminders.

Talking to your brain works well. You remind the brain that the pain does not come from a real injury but from an emotional strain. Handling the strain clears the pain faster than any physical therapy.

Getting active again matters too. You may have linked movement to pain, but the right move is to do more, not less. Physiotherapy can help when it is paced. Pick activities you have come to fear, start with them, and slowly loosen the grip of pain.

While you add activity, you should drop standard physiotherapy that only treats the body, since it will not solve the problem in the long run. Doing the things that used to hurt is more of a mental shift than a physical one, because it targets the learned link rather than the muscle.

If none of that helps, try a group.

A slow recovery usually means the person has not accepted the diagnosis. Sitting with other people who share the problem and who are getting better can give the courage needed to start healing.

A quick note: in Dr. Sarno's experience, normal cases of TMS clear up in two to six weeks.

chapters.title: Since TMS comes from the mind, psychotherapy can help
chapters.text:
Psychotherapy is still the main treatment for TMS, because the condition comes from the mind rather than from any physical injury. Patients are told to seek therapy instead of trusting in pills or exercise routines they saw on TV.

People with TMS often have a long history of rough emotions they have never let out.

Those emotions can be so heavy that even a good therapist has to work hard to loosen them. The job of therapy is to help the patient face the feelings rather than run from them. The patient has to accept that the events that caused the feelings are real, that they belong to the past, and that they cannot hurt anymore. For someone who lost a loved one and carries the weight, therapy can walk them away from the pain, slowly and steadily, in a way that works.

Research shows that only about five percent of patients need psychotherapy, which is why so few people look for it when they feel stuck.

A few structural conditions get diagnosed by mistake as TMS. It helps to know them, since they are the usual labels for cases that may actually be TMS. Sharp lower back pain gets called a herniated disc, even though by age twenty the part of the spine in question is no longer there to slip. In rare cases, a real structural problem does cause pain, and spinal stenosis is one, but it is uncommon. Even that one gets misdiagnosed as TMS sometimes.

Plenty of structural diagnoses for muscle, nerve, tendon, and ligament pain have no real physical cause. The mind is overlooking TMS and reading what it expects to read. To skip costly and risky procedures, a patient should have a mental check first to see if the pain is real or emotional. Serious treatment should only follow once repressed emotion has been ruled out, so the symptoms do not get worse.

chapters.title: The mind and body work together, and that is the key to healing TMS
chapters.text:
The earlier sections made clear that the mind shapes the body's health more than people admit, so it is worth laying out the full mind-body link as it relates to TMS.

The mind can act on the heart and blood vessels in dangerous ways. Stroke and death are on the list of risks. None of it comes from a physical cause, and that is the strange part. The mind is doing it, but how?

The mind holds the conscious and unconscious thoughts that guide behavior and expectation.

The autonomic nervous system kicks in when brain chemistry tips out of balance, and the body responds in uneven ways. Picture a moment when you lose a job or work turns tense. The mind can cut the brain off from oxygen through the heart, and a stroke follows.

The immune system is another place the mind acts directly. The immune system guards the body from outside and inside threats. It used to be seen as running on its own, but newer research shows the mind can shape it. There are cases where people feel pain or fall sick because their mind connected an event to the reaction. A useful example: dirty water brings cholera only with steady exposure, but once the mind knows the usual response, it can replay that response after a small dose that would not normally cause illness.

The mind can also push the immune system to do more. There are cases where people get well without medicine because the mind nudged the system to fight harder.

The mind-body link shows up in many other places. The mind can drive acne, warts, or a weak bowel that cannot hold spicy food or milk. The role of the mind gets downplayed, and that is a shame, because leaning on it would help a lot more people.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Today, you hear about a sore back or aching joint at every turn, and the usual suspect is some hard labor or a bad accident. The truth is that many of these aches come from Tension Myositis Syndrome. TMS is the body acting out the emotions the mind refuses to face.

Before you cut out every activity you have tagged as a trigger, talk to a psychotherapist to see what is really going on under the pain. If the cause is structural, then physical therapy is the right path.

There is no real medical treatment for TMS besides psychotherapy, but some patients cannot accept that simple talk can fix their pain. For those who demand a pill, a placebo can help. That means pills or routines with no medical value that still make the patient feel better. With TMS, the problem lives in the mind, and the fix has to involve the mind. A placebo works for the patient in a way a therapist alone cannot, because the patient believes the pain is physical and credits a brace or a workout for the relief. Whatever works is fine. The mind accepts the fix, and the body follows.

The mind also acts on the immune system in ways that show up as allergies and overreaction. There are cases where someone reacts badly to a drug for no medical reason, and yet the physical reaction is very real. An overactive immune system can lead to autoimmune disease, where the body attacks itself. Lupus and rheumatoid arthritis are on that list. Train the mind and the body will follow.

A small practice to try: talk back to your brain. It works well against the feelings that feed back pain.

Write out a few positive statements that push back against the wrong beliefs stored in your subconscious.

Over the next few weeks, repeat those statements to yourself as often as you can, until they settle into your subconscious.

We are what we believe.

Finished reading? Nice work.
